# Questions in Livestream 220

Live Stream #220: Bears, Bears, Everywheres

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:00</a> | What about the inscription Michael Barron thinks comes from Moses, even though it lacks a he and dates to the 18th century BC? | He says the channel will cover it in detail over the next two weeks and is already very critical of Barron's research. | He identifies the question as one already queued for upcoming coverage related to the Cameron Bertuzzi and Michael Barron material. He does not answer the inscription claim in detail here, but says it will be treated at length in the next two livestreams and signals that his evaluation of Barron's case is sharply negative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=1341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:21</a> | Can information in the Liber Pontificalis be used, or should it be distrusted? | He treats it cautiously at best, arguing that obvious forgeries in the work make it unreliable as a straightforward source. | He explains that the book gives early lives of the popes but opens, even in a sympathetic translation, with letters the translator admits are obvious forgeries. Because forged framing material is built into the work, he is unwilling to treat it as a simple reliable source and says its claims need heavy caution rather than automatic trust. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=1812" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:12</a> | Why should font choice matter in writing and literature? | Typography affects readability, tone, and the emotional force of the page. | He says fonts are important because they affect both readability and mood. For example, he says Palatino worked for his novels because it was readable while giving the page a sense of airiness and mystery, so the typeface should fit the medium and the emotional effect the writer wants. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=1977" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:57</a> | Should morality always be placed above practicality when societies mandate solutions? | Not always; he argues governments sometimes tolerate lesser evils for practical stability. | He argues that governments sometimes regulate or tolerate practices that are not morally ideal because banning every immoral thing can produce revolt or worse outcomes. He uses Moses permitting divorce because of hard hearts and American Prohibition as examples where practical governance and moral aspiration are not identical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=2173" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:13</a> | Does cultural homogenization lead to the death of individuality? | Only if it becomes extreme and totalizing; ordinary homogenization does not erase individuality by itself. | He says homogenization can damage individuality only when it becomes very extreme or controls every aspect of life. A culturally, ethnically, and linguistically homogeneous society can still have individual variation, especially when its common customs grow gradually rather than being imposed by fiat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=2335" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:55</a> | What work of fiction best captures despair? | He does not offer a firm pick and says he does not usually read books by looking for that theme. | He says the question is hard because despair is not a theme he seeks out when choosing fiction. He prefers stories that are simply good stories; if despair appears, that may be part of the story, but he does not have a personal recommendation built around that theme. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=2470" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:10</a> | What does 1 Peter 5:8 mean, especially the devil seeking to devour someone? | He reads it as a warning about spiritual destruction and the loss of faith, not literal chewing. | He reads the verse in the context of Peter's exhortations to humility, watchfulness, and firmness in faith. The devil's devouring is explained as drawing someone into sin, abandoning the faith, or leaving the path of salvation, so the answer is spiritual destruction rather than a literal metaphysical act of eating. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=2772" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:12</a> | Is there any reason to get a DMin? | He says its main practical use for a pastor is the title and potential pay increase; it is not a research doctorate. | He describes the DMin as a professional doctorate with a short program structure and a relatively limited thesis requirement. In his view it usually does not make someone a better preacher or pastor, but may give a pulpit pastor the title of doctor and a salary bump. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=2846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:26</a> | Could someone teach at a university with a DMin? | No; he says it is not a research doctorate and is not equivalent to a PhD for that role. | He repeats that the DMin is not a research doctorate and says universities do not treat it like a PhD for academic teaching. Its practical value is pastoral and credentialing-related, not qualifying someone for standard university faculty work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=3000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:00</a> | What are some tips for studying the prophets? | Start by learning biblical poetry, since prophetic books often speak poetically. | He recommends beginning with biblical poetry, especially Robert Alter's work on the art of biblical poetry, because prophetic books often communicate in poetic forms. Without understanding how biblical poetry works, a reader is likely to miss how the prophets structure their language and meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=3032" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:32</a> | Is a DMin any better than an honorary doctorate? | Somewhat; he treats a DMin as at least a professional doctorate, while an honorary doctorate does not really let someone call themselves doctor. | He distinguishes a DMin from an honorary degree by saying the DMin is at least an earned professional doctorate, however limited. An honorary doctorate, in his view, should not be used as a real title, while a DMin can explain why some ministers use the title even though it is not equivalent to a PhD. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=3164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:44</a> | How should someone answer the claim that saving faith in Jesus means keeping Old Testament law as before? | He calls that Judaizing and argues the old temple-centered law cannot be kept in that way and is fulfilled in Christ. | He says the claim turns Christian faith back into Judaizing because much of the old covenant law depended on temple, priesthood, and sacrificial structures that no longer operate in the same way. He argues Christians keep the law through its fulfillment in Christ, not by trying to recreate the pre-Christ temple-centered system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=3340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:40</a> | Are any other Robert Alter books worth a look? | He only strongly recommends The Art of Biblical Poetry; he says Alter's work on biblical prose or narrative is okay but not as strong. | He says Alter's poetry book is the one he most strongly recommends because it is especially useful for biblical studies. He is less enthusiastic about Alter's work on prose and narrative, describing it as acceptable but not as valuable as the poetry volume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=4602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:42</a> | Would it make more sense to sell your books directly from your own website instead of through Amazon? | He says no for their situation because Canadian shipping costs make direct fulfillment unattractive. | He says direct sales sound attractive but do not work well for them because they would have to handle fulfillment from Canada. Shipping costs and logistics would make it worse than selling through Amazon, where buyers already look for books and reviews also help visibility. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=4829" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:29</a> | What do you think of the claim that scholars found Moses's signature? | He is strongly skeptical and treats it as a fringe claim rather than a solid scholarly finding. | He treats the claim as part of the Barron-related material he plans to critique and is not persuaded that it represents mainstream scholarship. His comments frame it as an overreaching claim about an inscription rather than credible evidence that Moses's personal signature has been identified. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=5849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:29</a> | Have Western Christians tried to suppress or subvert Eastern Christianity? | He frames it more as neglect and ignorance than as a simple organized suppression campaign. | He says Western Christians have often ignored Eastern Christianity or treated it as unfamiliar, but he does not reduce the issue to a single coordinated suppression narrative. His answer emphasizes historical ignorance, Western focus on its own traditions, and the need to know Eastern Christianity better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=6659" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:59</a> | Could the Tower of Babel story refer to the collapse of the Akkadian Empire and a slow linguistic drift? | He says that is possible in broad outline but too uncertain to pin down confidently to that period. | He allows that the broad idea of imperial collapse and language separation could be related to the kind of world behind Babel, but he is cautious about identifying it specifically with the Akkadian collapse. The transcript supports possibility, not certainty, because the story is too compressed to anchor securely to one historical event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=6743" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:23</a> | Is there any connection between Babel and Babylon? | Only in the shared idea of bab as "gate"; he says that does not prove the Babel story is set in Babylon. | He explains that there is a linguistic connection around the element bab, meaning gate, but he separates that from proving a full Babylon identification. For him, the shared wordplay is real but not enough by itself to locate the narrative in later Babylon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=6833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:53</a> | Is there an etymological connection between babbling and the Tower of Babel? | Yes through the English translation tradition around Babel and confused language, not because the Hebrew word directly means confusion. | He says the English connection between Babel and babbling comes through the translation and reception tradition around confused speech. He does not say that the Hebrew word itself simply means babbling; the link is more about how the story was rendered and remembered in English. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=6927" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:27</a> | Did Joseph actually use a divining cup in Genesis 44:5? | Yes; he thinks Joseph used one, as other high officials in that setting did. | He says Joseph probably did have a divining cup and treats it as an authentic high-status object rather than only a narrative prop. He even shows or refers to a real example of such a cup to explain that this was a known kind of elite practice in the ancient setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=7222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:22</a> | Was Joseph's practice of divination illicit or sinful? | No, not at that point; he places it under a pre-Sinai patriarchal exemption. | He argues that Joseph lives before the Sinai law, so the later legal prohibition cannot simply be projected backward without qualification. He places Joseph with other patriarchal figures who do things later regulated or forbidden, and says this makes the practice not illicit for Joseph in that stage of redemptive history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=7449" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:09</a> | Could Pharaoh's obstinance be explained because the plagues resembled natural events seen before in Egypt? | Partly yes; familiar flood-related phenomena may have made them easier to rationalize, even though their scale was abnormal. | He says some plagues resemble things Egyptians already knew from Nile flood cycles, disease, insects, and natural disruption, so Pharaoh could rationalize them as unusually bad versions of familiar events. That does not make them ordinary, but it helps explain why Pharaoh might resist acknowledging them as divine judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=7717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:37</a> | Did Moses actually want Pharaoh to let Israel go for just a few days, or was it a way to make Pharaoh angry? | He says the request had Egyptian parallels and was not merely provocation, but it exposed Pharaoh's distrust and refusal. | He says the request for a temporary festival or journey was not simply a trick to enrage Pharaoh, since similar permissions had Egyptian parallels. At the same time, the request forced Pharaoh to show whether he trusted Moses and Israel at all, so his refusal exposed his suspicion and hardness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=8013" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:33</a> | Is Mary's perpetual virginity required, and why? | He says it is required only in churches that later dogmatized it, not across all Christian traditions. | He says perpetual virginity becomes required where a church has made it dogma, but it is not equally binding across all Christian traditions. He treats it as a later doctrinal commitment for some communions rather than something every Christian tradition must accept in the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=8224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:04</a> | Is it heretical to reject Theotokos when the term is properly defined? | Yes; he says rejecting it denies what Christians mean about Christ's full personhood. | He says Theotokos is not mainly about elevating Mary but about protecting the claim that the one born from her is the full person of Christ. If someone rejects the term after it is properly defined, he says that rejection cuts against orthodox Christology by dividing Christ's person. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=8312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:32</a> | Is Mary giving birth only to the flesh of Jesus? | No; he insists she gives birth to the full person of Christ, who is fully God and fully man. | He rejects the idea that Mary gives birth only to a body or only to Christ's human nature. His point is that birth is of a person, and the person born is Jesus Christ, fully human and fully divine, so the statement must not divide the person from the natures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=8590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:10</a> | What are your general thoughts on Karl Barth? | He is critical of Barth's neo-orthodoxy and some aspects of Barth's personal life. | He gives a generally negative assessment, criticizing Barth's neo-orthodox theology and also mentioning concerns about his personal conduct. The answer is not a detailed review of Barth's entire corpus, but it makes clear that he is wary of Barth as a theological model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=8902" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:22</a> | Do you know about Redeemed Zoomer, and can denominations be discussed lovingly and unitedly? | He has heard the name but does not watch much of him; he agrees denominations can be discussed respectfully and constructively. | He says he knows of Redeemed Zoomer but does not follow the channel closely enough to assess it in detail. On the broader point, he agrees that denominational differences can be discussed in a charitable way when the goal is understanding rather than factional hostility. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=9072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:12</a> | Why do so many problematic theologians seem to come from Germany? | He ties that partly to the structure of German education and the philosophical currents that shaped it. | He connects Germany's influence to its educational structures and to philosophical movements that shaped academic theology. His answer does not say Germany uniquely produces bad theology, but that German universities and intellectual traditions had outsized influence on modern critical and speculative theological trends. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=9341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:41</a> | What does the second part of Matthew 10:23 mean? | He reads it as Jesus saying the disciples will not finish that Israel-focused mission before the Son of Man comes. | He takes the statement in its immediate mission-to-Israel context and reads it as saying the disciples will not complete that work before the Son of Man comes. He later clarifies that he does not prefer an AD 70 reading for this verse, leaning instead toward a resurrection-related fulfillment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=9355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:55</a> | Have you gotten out of the shadowban yet? | No; he says YouTube shut off creator email notifications, which especially hurts smaller channels that depend on community engagement. | He says they have not really gotten out of the problem and describes YouTube changes that prevent email notifications from reaching subscribers. In his view, that disproportionately harms smaller channels because they rely on direct audience notification and community response. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=9896" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:56</a> | Could Matthew 10:23 and Matthew 16:27-28 refer to AD 70? | He does not think so; he reads Matthew 10:23 more toward resurrection and Matthew 16:27-28 toward the transfiguration. | He rejects the AD 70 explanation for both passages in this exchange. He says Matthew 10:23 is better connected to the resurrection, while Matthew 16:27-28 points toward the transfiguration because the following narrative shows some disciples seeing Christ's glory before death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=10076" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:56</a> | Would Ruth 4:18-22 count as evidence for a late Exodus date? | He says yes, if the genealogy is taken as a short line from the Exodus period down to David. | He says Ruth's genealogy can support a late Exodus if the line is read as relatively short and continuous from the Exodus era to David. He also recognizes that biblical genealogies can be selective, so the force of the evidence depends on how compressed the genealogy is judged to be. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=10272" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:12</a> | Are there good reasons to radically revise ancient Near Eastern chronology? | No; he argues the current chronology rests on a very large body of evidence. | He says ancient Near Eastern chronology is not built on one fragile datum but on many mutually reinforcing pieces of evidence. Because the system is heavily cross-checked, he thinks radical revisions usually ignore how much evidence would have to move together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=10460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:20</a> | Why are Matthew's and Luke's genealogies different, and does 1 Timothy 1:4 apply here? | He treats them as serving different purposes, one legal and one bloodline, and says 1 Timothy is not about Christ's pedigree. | He says the New Testament genealogies are not the kind of speculative genealogical obsession warned against in 1 Timothy. He explains the differences by appealing to distinct purposes and lines, commonly understood as legal descent through Joseph and a genealogical or bloodline connection through Mary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=10775" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:35</a> | Does the word heaven have multiple meanings in the Bible? | Yes; it can mean sky, celestial realm, or the spiritual heaven depending on context. | He says biblical language about heaven can refer to the visible sky, the realm of stars or celestial bodies, and the spiritual heaven. Context determines the sense, so readers should not flatten every occurrence into one meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=10980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:00</a> | Have you ever considered creating an Ancient Egypt and the Bible board game? | No; he has thought more about video games and says board games are expensive to produce and market. | He says he has not seriously considered a board game and is more likely to think about video game possibilities. Board games require physical components, manufacturing, and marketing, which makes them expensive and logistically unattractive for their situation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=11341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:01</a> | What are some DM tips after running a first D&D one-shot? | Plan ahead, expect players to miss things, and prepare surprises or complications in advance. | He says good DMing requires planning, because players may overlook clues, derail expected paths, or solve problems unpredictably. A DM should prepare extra complications and surprises so the session still has momentum even when the players do not follow the intended route. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=11520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:00</a> | How do you get into D&D, and what do you need to play? | Learn the rules, get dice or electronic dice, and treat it as collaborative roleplaying rather than a board game. | He describes D&D as a roleplaying game rather than a normal board game. New players need to learn the basic rules, have dice or an electronic substitute, and understand that the point is collaborative imagination under a DM rather than moving pieces around a fixed board. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=11712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:12</a> | Do you make your own D&D character for the game? | Yes, but within the campaign limits the host sets for class, alignment, and level. | He says players generally make their own characters, but the DM or host sets campaign boundaries. Those limits may include what classes are allowed, what alignments fit the game, and what level the character should begin at. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=11808" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:48</a> | If someone is studying to be a worship leader, can they play a bard in the D&D game? | Maybe; he likes the idea and says he may open the game to clerics and bards. | He reacts positively to the idea and says he may expand the allowed character options. The answer is tentative because it depends on the specific game limits, but he seems open to including bards and possibly clerics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=11872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:52</a> | Does Ahura Mazda count as pagan for the no-pagan-deities rule? | Yes; he says it does and that he would not treat Zoroastrian gods as valid for that rule. | He says Ahura Mazda would count as pagan for the rule he is applying in the game. The restriction is not limited to Greco-Roman deities, so Zoroastrian divine figures would not be valid exceptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=11963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:23</a> | Was Genesis 49:8-10 originally aimed at Moses's time, or does it come from the monarchy? | He resists a monarchy-only reading because the Shiloh or messianic element reaches beyond that setting. | He does not reduce the Judah oracle to a simple monarchy-period origin. He says the text may speak into royal expectations, but the reference often associated with Shiloh or messianic hope reaches beyond an ordinary political setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=12203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:23</a> | Which tribe was Samson from? | Dan. | He gives the direct answer without elaboration: Samson was from the tribe of Dan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=12420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:00</a> | In 2 Corinthians 12, is the third heaven the spiritual heaven, or one layer among several heavens? | He says the text does not settle it clearly, but it may point to a higher level within a more complex heavenly realm. | He says Paul does not define the third heaven in a way that settles the geography with certainty. He allows for a more complex heavenly realm with levels or regions, and suggests the third heaven may be the highest or a special place within that broader heavenly geography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=12790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:10</a> | Why do the genealogies in Matthew and Luke differ? | He argues Christ inherits through two lines, one legal and one genealogical. | He explains the difference as two genealogies serving two functions: one establishes legal descent through Joseph and the royal line, while the other can be understood as Mary's line or a bloodline connection. He also notes that biblical genealogies often serve specific theological and legal purposes rather than simply listing every biological link. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=13241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:41</a> | Will a Numbers book be next after the plagues book? | Probably not; he says he wants to do a synchronisms book first, while another science-fiction draft is also in progress. | He says Numbers is not likely to be next because he wants to work on a synchronisms book first. He also mentions other writing projects, including another science-fiction draft, so the answer is more about his current project queue than a rejection of Numbers permanently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=13489" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:49</a> | Which fonts have you used in your books so far? | He says he uses Palatino Linotype in the novels. | He says Palatino Linotype is the font used in the novels, chosen because it fit the reading experience they wanted. In nearby comments he contrasts it with other fonts such as Garamond and explains that font choice affects readability and tone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=13690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:10</a> | What is the best font for comics? | He prefers a brush font unless someone is hand-lettering. | He says he would generally use a brush-style font for comics unless the lettering is done by hand. He also notes that the best font depends on the comic's style and mood, because the lettering should fit the artwork rather than applying one universal comic font. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=13802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:02</a> | Which fonts does David Falk recommend for PowerPoint? | He recommends Frutiger because it is clear at large and small sizes and also works well for maps and video text. | He recommends Frutiger as an excellent general-purpose font because it remains clear at both large presentation sizes and very small map labels. He also uses it for video text, describing it as one of the clearest fonts available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=14016" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:36</a> | Has David ever made a comic book? | Yes; he drew an unpublished comic and started another, but says comic art is very slow to produce. | He says he has drawn a comic book but never published it, and also began another called Varney the Vampire. The main reason he does not pursue that work more is the time cost: drawing, inking, and finishing comic art takes him a very long time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=14361" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:21</a> | If you had to learn a language from the Indian subcontinent, which would you pick and why? | He would pick Sanskrit, partly because of its appearance on some Japanese swords. | He says he would choose Sanskrit, not primarily for professional reasons, but because of his interest in Japanese swords as works of art. Since Sanskrit inscriptions sometimes appear on blades, learning the language would help him understand those inscriptions in a hobby context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=14557" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:37</a> | Why do some Japanese swords have Sanskrit on them? | He links it to Buddhist influence on Japanese sword culture, with some inscriptions also serving decorative or practical uses. | He traces the practice to Buddhist influence in Japan, including monks and swordsmiths who incorporated Buddhist motifs and Sanskrit sayings into sword craft. He adds that some inscriptions were decorative, while others could also cover defects in a blade. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=14656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:16</a> | Why did Christianity become a minority in Japan? | Because it was outlawed and violently persecuted after an earlier period of strong growth. | He says Christianity had once grown strongly in Japan, including among samurai, because themes such as lordship and fidelity made cultural sense. It later became a persecuted minority because it was outlawed and suppressed violently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=14721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:21</a> | Is Christianity too pacifist for samurai? | He says no; at that time Christianity did not require samurai to stop being samurai, and divine lordship could appeal to them. | He says Christianity at that time did not demand that samurai stop being warriors, so it was not inherently too pacifist for them. He argues that the idea of ultimate divine lordship in Christ could actually appeal to a culture already shaped by loyalty and obedience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=14859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:39</a> | Do people make modern recreations of Christian Japanese sword fittings? | Not really for the scarce tsuba he wants; he says some modern Christian swords exist, but they look fake. | He says people may make modern Christian-themed swords, but not credible recreations of the specific Christian Japanese fittings he wants. He describes those historical tsuba as extremely hard to obtain and says modern versions tend to look fake. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qYRbD6EriCg?t=15048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:48</a> | Are Japanese sword shows fun and extremely expensive? | He says they are fun and not necessarily expensive, since entry was cheap and small accessories let budget collectors participate. | He says sword shows can be fun without requiring a large blade-buying budget. Collectors with less money could focus on smaller items such as handguards or menuki, sometimes buying inexpensive pieces while still participating in the collecting world. |
